task_instructions: ### Objective    This task evaluates how easy it is for prospective postgraduate students to create an account, which is a required step before starting an application.    It explores whether participants can clearly understand what is required, access and interpret key information such as terms and conditions, and navigate the account creation process in an accessible and intuitive way.    ### Note    You will be interacting with a realistic version of the UQ account creation experience. It mirrors the real enrolment process, but it is a safe test environment created for research purposes.    Please use made up details when completing the form. Do not use your real name, email address or personal information.    This task finishes once you reach the screen that allows you to search for programs. The focus is only on account creation.    ### Real-World User Scenario    You have decided to enrol in a Master of Business at UQ. To begin the application process, you must first create an account.    Record your screen and talk through your experience after clicking the link below. You will need to commence this task by **Registering an Account**    * â€?Share your first impressions of the page. Is it clear what you need to do?    â€?Select a section of the terms and conditions as if you were genuinely considering reading them. Are they clear and easy to understand, or overwhelming? How could they be improved?    â€?Complete the account creation form using made up details.    â€?Explain whether the form is easy to understand and interact with. Highlight anything confusing, unclear or difficult to use.
finding: Participant found the terms and conditions written in formal legal language and concluded they were not user-friendly enough to read in full.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled through the terms and conditions section and paused without engaging deeply with the content.
stated_signal: Written in formal legal language, it's not very user-friendly, and I wouldn't realistically read all of it in detail.
rationale: Participant explicitly identified the terms and conditions as formally written and not user-friendly, leading them to skip detailed reading. Observed scrolling behaviour aligns with stated disengagement. No strong frustration expressed; tone is matter-of-fact. Matches L2: hesitation and non-optimal engagement, task not blocked.

task_instructions: ### Objective    This task evaluates how easy it is for logged-in users to locate security settings within their profile and enrol in Multi-Factor Authentication. It explores clarity, usability, accessibility and user understanding of MFA within the enrolment portal.    ### Real-World User Scenario    You recently heard that one of the online services you use experienced a data breach. Cyber Security is now top of mind for you. As a result, you have decided to strengthen your security across every digital service you use.     You are now logged into your UQ student account and want to enable Multi-Factor Authentication to better protect your information.    Record your screen and talk through your experience.    ### Before you begin:    * In your own words, explain what Multi-Factor Authentication means to you.  * What is your preferred type of MFA, if any? For example, an authenticator app, SMS, email, push notification or something else.   * If you use an authenticator app, which one and why do you prefer it?    ### Then:    * Try to find where you would enrol in MFA from the logged-in student application dashboard. Is it easy to locate security or profile settings?  * Attempt to enrol in MFA. Can you do it successfully?  * Is the process clear and easy to follow? Explain any confusion, friction or accessibility barriers you experience.  * Explain what works well or suggest how the experience could be improved and explain why those changes would help.
finding: Participant found the MFA enrolment process overly complex, requiring app installation, QR code scanning, and code entry, and questioned how ordinary users would manage it.
observed_signal: Participant read through the MFA steps, paused repeatedly, and began checking whether they already had Google Authenticator installed rather than proceeding with installation.
stated_signal: I need to install something which is already a pain. How is a person going to input something like this and remember where they were?
rationale: Participant explicitly identified the multi-step MFA process as excessive effort, requiring app installation before even reaching the QR scan step. Strong verbal frustration ("already a pain", "Oh my God") aligns with observed hesitation and avoidance behaviour. Task not abandoned but participant is struggling. Matches L3: significant friction, frustration expressed, task still in progress.

task_instructions: ### **Objective**    Downloadable resources often donâ€™t maintain accessibility in a way thatâ€™s consistent with the homepage. This task aims to evaluate the usability, clarity, and accessibility of the WA Governmentâ€™s downloadable fact sheet on coercive control. Participants will assess whether the PDF effectively explains coercive control, provides practical guidance on how to help someone, and is accessible and usable for people with diverse abilities, literacy levels, and assistive technologies. The task will surface opportunities to improve content clarity, document structure, accessibility compliance, and overall usefulness.    ### Task     Open and review the following fact sheet available via the website. Please read through the document from beginning to end.    As you review it, talk through your thoughts and reactions. Please comment on:    * Whether it is clear who the fact sheet is for and what it aims to help people do  * Whether it clearly explains what coercive control is and how to help someone  * Anything that feels confusing, unclear, or difficult to understand  * Please also describe whether you would save or share this document in a real situation    If you use accessibility tools (such as screen readers, zoom, or keyboard navigation), please comment on how well the document works with them.
finding: Participant using a screen reader could not access any content in the PDF fact sheet, receiving only repetitive empty group labels with no meaningful content announced.
observed_signal: Screen reader output consisted entirely of repeated "empty group, page one containing" announcements with no actual document content read aloud. Participant considered using a phone OCR app as a workaround.
stated_signal: I've got no access to the content in here at all.
rationale: Participant's screen reader returned only structural noise with no readable content from the PDF, constituting a complete accessibility failure for this task component. Participant explicitly confirmed total loss of access and began considering an external OCR workaround, indicating near-abandonment. Matches L4: severe friction with near-abandonment. S2 rather than S1 because the overall session outcome may still be achievable via workaround, but this task component is fully blocked.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is a screen reader user. PDF inaccessibility is structurally amplifying for this cohort â€?a sighted user would have full access to the same document with no friction. The barrier is entirely cohort-specific.
notes: Classic PDF accessibility failure â€?no alt text, no tagged structure readable by screen reader. Strong candidate for Step 8.3 case study.

task_instructions: ### **Objective**    This task aims to evaluate how clearly the AAMIÂ  homepage communicates the organisationâ€™s role, purpose, and value to users seeking information about home insurance. Participants will assess whether the homepage helps them understand what AAMI does, who it serves, and where to go next. The task will also surface accessibility, usability, and design factors that affect how easily users can interpret and navigate key content areas.    ## Instructions     Take your time with this one...from top to bottom, go through the AAMI website narrating your thoughts.     What information are you drawn to and why?    Is there anything that is confusing or that you don't understand? point it out.    How could it be improved?
finding: Participant using VoiceOver had to navigate the homepage line by line due to the volume of content, making it significantly more effortful than heading-based navigation would allow.
observed_signal: Participant described moving through the page line by line rather than by headings, indicating the page structure did not support efficient AT navigation.
stated_signal: For someone using voiceover, there's a lot to listen to. I'm just going line by line at the moment.
rationale: Participant explicitly identified the homepage as burdensome for VoiceOver users due to content density and lack of efficient heading structure. Line-by-line navigation confirms the AT experience is significantly degraded compared to sighted browsing. Tone is analytical rather than distressed. Matches L3: significant friction requiring sustained effort, task not abandoned.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is a VoiceOver user. Dense homepage content with insufficient heading structure is structurally amplifying for screen reader users who cannot visually skim â€?sighted users can scan the same page in seconds.
notes: Window contains two findings â€?see Finding 2 for F4 scroll control issue.

Youâ€™re specifically interested in undertaking study at a universityÂ within Australia rather than a TAFE or broader certification program.    1. Describe what you would want to know before you enrol in a university degree or single university course?  2. Talk through the search terms or phrases you would use to find information on universities, in Australia that offer courses and programs related to your preferred topic of study  3. Record your screen and describe how you would begin your search for information to determine which university is right for you.  4. Based on your search results, which websites or results stand out to you and why?  5. Share your first impressions of the results and whether any specific university webpages capture your attention
finding: Participant was interrupted by download or input prompts before having time to review the landing page, and questioned the legitimacy of the website.

stated_signal: The thing that really bothers me is when they try and have you download things or input things that you haven't even had the time to look at the landing page. Get off my back.
rationale: Participant encountered unexpected prompts before being able to review the page, matching F5 definition. Strong verbal frustration confirms negative emotional response. Secondary F2 signal present but dominant trigger is unexpected interruption behaviour. Matches L2: hesitation and non-optimal path, task not abandoned.
notes: Secondary F2 signal: participant questioned site legitimacy. Primary label F5 based on unexpected prompt behaviour.
